nativeRange.habitat: Flowing, cool, shallow streams and hill tributaries; substrates of small rocks with patches of sand; found in the Raidak River system (Brahmaputra tributary) and streams of Buxa National Park and the western Garo Hills

decorPreferences: small caves (coconut shells, clay pots, PVC tubes) for breeding and shelter
dense planting (Java fern, Anubias, mosses) for broken lines of sight
driftwood and leaf litter for naturalistic cover

diet.notes: Obligate micropredator; strongly prefers live or frozen micro-foods — Artemia (brine shrimp), Daphnia, glassworm (Chaoborus), and Cyclops. Many individuals refuse dried or pelleted foods entirely, especially on first introduction. Avoid bloodworm and Tubifex as a dietary staple due to obesity and disease risk. Feed small portions 2–3 times daily. Weaning onto high-quality frozen foods is achievable with patience but live food should always be available as a fallback.

goodWith: small loaches kept in separate territory zones
similarly-sized calm gouramis in larger tanks
avoidWith: other male Badis blosyrus (intense conspecific aggression in small tanks)
other Badis species (hybridisation risk and competition)
Neocaridina and Caridina shrimp (predated, especially juveniles)
fast-moving or boisterous fish that outcompete for food

summary: Small cryptic micropredator from cool NE India hill streams (Assam, West Bengal, Meghalaya). Males display dramatic courtship colour changes. Often exported mixed with the near-identical B. assamensis — distinguished by a deeper mouth cleft. Intermediate care: live-food obligate and strongly territorial between males.
careNotes:
Badis blosyrus is a retiring, slow-moving benthic species that spends most of its time resting near the substrate or inside caves and dense vegetation. Males are highly aggressive toward conspecific rivals and will fight intensely in small tanks; a single male per aquarium is safest in a 60 L setup, or a species group can be maintained in a larger tank (100 L+) with numerous caves and broken sightlines ensuring each male holds a clear territory. A pair is the most common and reliable keeper configuration.

This species is a live-food near-obligate: wild fish feed on small invertebrates and micro-crustaceans, and many captive specimens are reluctant to accept frozen foods initially, let alone dried pellets. A steady supply of live Artemia nauplii, Daphnia, and Cyclops is strongly recommended, particularly for newly acquired individuals. Frozen equivalents may be accepted once the fish is settled. Bloodworms and Tubifex are palatable but should be used sparingly as they contribute to fatty-liver disease over time.

The species belongs to the Badis species complex — numerous cryptic species exist across South and Southeast Asia, many described only recently (Kullander & Britz 2002 revision). Badis blosyrus is easily confused with B. assamensis; both species are exported mixed from northeast India and careful examination of mouth cleft depth is needed to distinguish them. Hobbyists should expect mixed bags.

Water quality must remain clean and well-oxygenated; moderate flow (4–5× turnover) suits the Raidak River biotope. The temperature tolerance skews cool (15–25 °C) relative to most tropical aquarium fish, making it a good candidate for unheated or cool tanks in temperate climates. Avoid sudden temperature swings. Perform regular small water changes (20–25% weekly).

Note: Badidae is no longer treated as a cichlid relative; molecular phylogenetics place it in Anabantiformes (with gouramis and snakeheads), though some older hobby sources still list order Perciformes.
breedingNotes: Cave spawner forming temporary pair bonds. The male courts the female with dramatic colour intensification and fin-spreading displays. Once a pair is established, the female lays 30–100 eggs inside a cave or sheltered cavity; the male then drives her away and provides sole parental care, fanning and guarding the eggs. Eggs hatch in approximately 2–3 days at 23–25 °C; fry become free-swimming at 6–8 days. At this point the male’s protective behaviour intensifies and it is advisable to remove the female to a separate tank. First foods: freshly hatched Artemia nauplii and micro-worms. Condition breeders thoroughly on live foods beforehand. Breeding is relatively straightforward once a compatible pair is established and live food is plentiful.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Badis blosyrus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (males 65–70 mm), temperature (15–25°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (54–179 ppm), tank footprint (75×30 cm minimum), diet (Artemia, Daphnia, glassworm; avoid bloodworm/Tubifex), breeding (cave spawner, 30–100 eggs, 2–3 day hatch, male-only care), native range (Assam/West Bengal/Meghalaya, Raidak River), taxonomy (Perciformes/Badidae), and conspecific aggression notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Badis blosyrus
additional.notes: Cross-checked max length (4.2 cm SL male), taxonomy (Order Anabantiformes per current molecular placement, Family Badidae), distribution (Asia: India), trophic level (3.2, carnivore), habitat (freshwater benthopelagic, tropical), IUCN Least Concern (2010). FishBase SL of 4.2 cm is consistent with ~5–6 cm TL reported elsewhere.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Badis assamensis and Badis blosyrus
additional.notes: Trade importer note confirming B. blosyrus and B. assamensis are exported mixed; distinguishing feature is the larger mouth cleft of B. blosyrus extending below the centre of the eye margin. Largest measured specimen confirmed at 6 cm TL. Notes both as cave breeders with male guarding spawn.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Badis blosyrus Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary care source. Confirmed 4–5 cm adult size, 5–8 year lifespan, pH 6.3–7.0, temperature 23–26°C, hardness 8–15°N, minimum tank 50 L, live/frozen food preference, cave-spawning breeding behaviour, and sexual dimorphism (males more colourful with elongated fins).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Badis blosyrus
additional.notes: Attempted fetch returned HTTP 403. Source listed as accessed per known publication record for this species (PFK has a documented species profile for B. blosyrus). Parameters consistent with other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave spawner; male provides sole parental care after driving female away. Clutch of 30–100 eggs hatches in 2–3 days; fry free-swimming at 6–8 days. Condition breeders on live food; remove female post-spawning. First foods: Artemia nauplii and micro-worms.
